source_profile: Solo sysadmin with strong backup philosophy

turns.content: My rule: every backup must be tested monthly by restoring to a staging environment. A backup you've never restored is not a backup, it's a hope.
Principle logged. Backup validity requires monthly restore tests to staging. Untested backups are not considered reliable.
I also never store backups only on the same cloud provider as production. If AWS has an outage and that's where your backups live too, you're done. I keep a copy in Backblaze B2.
Noted. Cross-provider backup policy: production on AWS, backup copy maintained in Backblaze B2. Single-provider backup is rejected as insufficient.

question: Where does this sysadmin keep their off-site backup copy and why?
expected_answer: Backblaze B2. The reason is to avoid single-cloud-provider risk — if AWS has an outage and backups are also on AWS, recovery is impossible.
atomic_facts: Production runs on AWS
Backup copy is stored in Backblaze B2
Cross-provider backup is required policy
Backups must be tested monthly via staging restore

source_profile: SRE investigating cascading failure

turns.content: We disabled connection pooling on the API servers last week to debug a weird timeout issue. Temporary measure. We're planning to re-enable it once we isolate the bug.

Major incident today. Database hit max_connections and started refusing connections. Checkout was down for 22 minutes. Turns out the pooling being off meant every API request opened a new DB connection directly.
Incident logged. Checkout down 22 minutes. Root cause: connection pooling disabled on API servers, causing direct per-request DB connections, exhausting max_connections limit.
We re-enabled pooling immediately. Connections dropped back to normal within 2 minutes.
Remediation noted. Pooling re-enabled, connections normalized within 2 minutes.

question: What caused the database max_connections exhaustion incident on March 8?

source_profile: Platform engineer dealing with storage cascade

turns.content: Logging retention is set to 90 days in our ELK stack. We've been running that for a year without issues. Disk usage has been steady around 60%.

We onboarded 3 new microservices last week. They're chatty — generating about 4x the log volume of our existing services. Didn't adjust the retention policy yet.
Noted. 3 new microservices added generating ~4x log volume vs existing services. Retention policy still at 90 days, not yet adjusted.
ELK cluster ran out of disk last night. Elasticsearch started dropping logs. The disk went from 60% to 97% in two weeks.
Disk exhaustion event logged. ELK went from 60% to 97% disk usage in two weeks, causing log drops. Elasticsearch started rejecting new log ingestion.
We're cutting retention to 30 days as immediate mitigation.
Mitigation applied. Log retention reduced from 90 days to 30 days.

expected_answer: Three new chatty microservices were onboarded around February 28, generating approximately 4x the normal log volume. The 90-day retention policy was not adjusted. Retention policy was not adjusted after onboarding
Disk went from 60% to 97% in two weeks

source_profile: On-call engineer investigating failed deployment

turns.content: We disabled health check enforcement in our CD pipeline last sprint. It was causing too many false deployment failures. Meant to re-enable it after fixing the health check logic but it slipped through.

We deployed a bad build today. The new pods were crash-looping but the deployment completed successfully because health checks aren't enforced. Traffic hit the bad pods for 11 minutes before we caught it manually.

Incident logged. Bad build deployed with crash-looping pods. Deployment completed without detecting failures because health check enforcement was disabled. 11 minutes of bad traffic before manual detection.
Re-enabling health checks immediately. This is exactly the scenario we disabled them to avoid from false positives and instead got the real thing.
Health check enforcement re-enabled. Root cause confirmed: disabling enforcement allowed a failing deployment to pass undetected.

expected_answer: The deployment succeeded without detection because health check enforcement had been disabled in the CD pipeline in early April. Without enforcement, the crash-looping pods didn't fail the deployment gate. Traffic hit the bad pods for 11 minutes before manual detection. The root cause was the disabled health checks.

Disable was meant to be temporary but was not re-enabled

source_profile: Startup CTO tracking performance regression

turns.content: We enabled verbose query logging in PostgreSQL two days ago. DEBUG1 level. It was for a performance audit. Planned to run it for a week then turn it off.
Logged. PostgreSQL verbose query logging enabled at DEBUG1 level as of March 18. Planned duration: one week.
API response times have degraded 40% over the past 5 days. P95 latency went from 120ms to 168ms. We can't figure out why — no code changes, no traffic increase.
Performance degradation noted. P95 latency: 120ms → 168ms (+40%). No code or traffic changes. Timeframe matches the PostgreSQL DEBUG1 logging enabled March 18.
Oh. Oh no. The verbose logging. We should turn that off immediately.
Confirmed hypothesis. DEBUG1 logging adds significant I/O overhead on write-heavy databases. Recommend disabling immediately and monitoring latency recovery.

question: What caused the 40% API latency increase starting around March 18, and what is the remediation?
expected_answer: The latency increase was caused by enabling PostgreSQL verbose query logging at DEBUG1 level on March 18. The additional I/O overhead from verbose logging degraded P95 latency from 120ms to 168ms. The remediation is to disable the DEBUG1 logging.
atomic_facts: PostgreSQL DEBUG1 query logging was enabled March 18, 2026
P95 latency increased from 120ms to 168ms (40% increase)
No code changes or traffic increase occurred during this period
The latency increase correlates with the DEBUG1 logging enablement
Disabling verbose logging is the remediation
